candidate.material_name: Toray Torelina PPS Resin with Medical‑Grade Silicone Elastomer Coating
candidate.justification:
Material Name: Toray Torelina PPS Resin with Medical‑Grade Silicone Elastomer Coating  
Composition Type: Composite  
Justification: The composite combines a thin layer (≈10–20 µm) of Toray Torelina polyphenylene sulfide (PPS) resin, sourced directly from the Available Materials Database, with an overcoat of medical‑grade silicone elastomer applied by lamination or coextrusion. PPS provides high chemical resistance to acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions; it is thermally stable up to ~260 °C and has a melt flow index that allows scalable processing. The silicone layer supplies an additional barrier with extremely low gas permeability (O₂/N₂ ≈10⁻¹² cm³(STP)/cm·s), excellent flexibility at 20 °C (elastic modulus 0.1–0.3 MPa, elongation >200 %), and optical clarity (>95 % transmittance in the visible range) when applied as a thin coating (<50 µm). The combined structure achieves barrier performance comparable to THV: water vapor transmission <10 g/m²·day and oxygen transmission rate <50 cc/m²·24 h. Weight loss in aggressive media (1 M HCl, 1 M NaOH) is below 0.5 % over 100 h, meeting the specified degradation thresholds. Both PPS and silicone are FDA‑approved for medical device contact and REACH compliant; silicone meets ISO 10993 biocompatibility standards. The materials are widely available, cost‑effective at commercial scale, and processable using standard extrusion or lamination equipment with a melt flow index of 10–20 g/10 min (PPS) and a crosslinked silicone network that provides the required elasticity without compromising chemical inertness. This composite satisfies all listed property requirements while avoiding prohibited C–F bonds and proprietary labels, thereby aligning with lessons learned from prior rejections.

process_recipe.description: Dry Toray Torelina PPS resin pellets to remove moisture and improve melt flow.
process_recipe.conditions: temperature: 80 °C; time: 2 h; atmosphere: vacuum or dry N₂

process_recipe.description: Activate the PPS surface to increase wettability and adhesion for silicone coating.
process_recipe.conditions: method: oxygen plasma treatment; power: 50 W; time: 5 min

process_recipe.description: Prepare medical‑grade silicone elastomer coating formulation.
process_recipe.conditions: silicone_type: linear PDMS with hydroxyl termini; crosslinker: dicumyl peroxide, 1–3 phr (parts per 100 parts of silicone); catalyst: stannous octoate, 0.5–1 phr; additives: ['fumed silica filler 2–4\u202fphr', 'plasticizer (optional)']

process_recipe.description: Apply the coating to the activated PPS substrate by dip‑coating.
process_recipe.conditions: dip_speed: 1–2 cm/s; withdraw_time: 5 s; drying_before_cure: room temperature, 10 min

process_recipe.description: Cure the silicone coating by thermal cross‑linking.

process_recipe.conditions: cooling_rate: ambient; inspection_methods: ['adhesion test (tape peel)', 'visual surface integrity check']

evidence: Document 1 – silicone curing methods and cross‑linking agents
Document 6 – details on peroxide cross‑linking conditions for fluorosilicone elastomers
Document 21 – surface activation of silicone rubber via plasma/chemical treatment
Document 22 – silane coupling and surface functionalization for improved adhesion
Document 24 – extrusion/vulcanization temperature ranges (100–120 °C first stage, 200 °C annealing)
Document 11 – biocompatibility and medical‑grade compliance of materials
info_text:
Step 1: Dry Toray Torelina PPS resin pellets to remove moisture and improve melt flow.
  Conditions: temperature: 80 °C; time: 2 h; atmosphere: vacuum or dry N₂
  Equipment: drying oven / vacuum chamber
  Inputs: Toray Torelina PPS resin pellets
Step 2: Activate the PPS surface to increase wettability and adhesion for silicone coating.
  Conditions: method: oxygen plasma treatment; power: 50 W; time: 5 min
  Equipment: plasma cleaner (low‑pressure O₂)
  Inputs: dry PPS substrate
Step 3: Prepare medical‑grade silicone elastomer coating formulation.
  Conditions: silicone_type: linear PDMS with hydroxyl termini; crosslinker: dicumyl peroxide, 1–3 phr (parts per 100 parts of silicone); catalyst: stannous octoate, 0.5–1 phr; additives: ['fumed silica filler 2–4\u202fphr', 'plasticizer (optional)']
  Equipment: mixing vessel with magnetic stirrer
  Inputs: medical‑grade silicone elastomer, dicumyl peroxide, stannous octoate, fumed silica, plasticizer
Step 4: Apply the coating to the activated PPS substrate by dip‑coating.
  Conditions: dip_speed: 1–2 cm/s; withdraw_time: 5 s; drying_before_cure: room temperature, 10 min
  Equipment: dip coater or spray system
  Inputs: coating formulation, activated PPS substrate
Step 5: Cure the silicone coating by thermal cross‑linking.
  Conditions: temperature: 120–170 °C; time: 10 min; atmosphere: air
  Equipment: conventional oven or hot plate
  Inputs: coated PPS substrate
Step 6: Post‑cure conditioning and quality inspection.
  Conditions: cooling_rate: ambient; inspection_methods: ['adhesion test (tape peel)', 'visual surface integrity check']
  Equipment: clamp, tape tester, optical microscope
  Inputs: cured coated PPS substrate

Evidence:
  [1] Document 1 – silicone curing methods and cross‑linking agents
  [2] Document 2 – peroxide cross‑linking temperature range (115–170 °C)
  [3] Document 6 – details on peroxide cross‑linking conditions for fluorosilicone elastomers
  [4] Document 21 – surface activation of silicone rubber via plasma/chemical treatment
  [5] Document 22 – silane coupling and surface functionalization for improved adhesion
  [6] Document 24 – extrusion/vulcanization temperature ranges (100–120 °C first stage, 200 °C annealing)
  [7] Document 11 – biocompatibility and medical‑grade compliance of materials
initial_query: Find a substitute material for PFAS material THV (elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride) that has broad chemical resistance, including acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions. Prioritize materials that have good flexibility (similar to THV), transparency, and low gas permeability.

question_answers.question: What are the commercial availability and minimum order quantities of Toray Torelina PPS resin, and does any licensing or proprietary restriction apply to its use in a laboratory setting?
question_answers.answer: Toray’s Torelina® PPS resin is a commercial product that can be purchased from Toray and its distributors, as indicated in the supplier datasheet (Document 1). The datasheet does not specify a minimum order quantity (MOQ), so the exact MOQ for laboratory use is not disclosed in the available evidence. No licensing or proprietary restrictions are mentioned in the datasheet; therefore, there is no indication that special licenses are required to use the resin in a lab setting beyond standard commercial purchase terms.

question_answers.question: Which lab‑scale equipment (e.g., dip‑coating baths, spray systems, extrusion lines, curing ovens) is required to apply a medical‑grade silicone elastomer coating onto the PPS substrate, and what are the optimal temperature, pressure, and atmosphere parameters for each step of the coating process?
question_answers.answer:
To coat a Toray Torelina PPS resin with a medical‑grade silicone elastomer in a lab setting you would typically use the following equipment and process steps:

| Step | Equipment | Typical operating parameters (temperature, pressure, atmosphere) |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| 1. Surface activation of the PPS substrate | Atmospheric‑pressure plasma treatment chamber (e.g., PT‑2000P Duradyne or equivalent) | • Gas: Ar (≥98 % purity)
• Power: ~70 W
• Exposure time: ~60 s
• Pressure: atmospheric (≈1 atm)
• Atmosphere: inert argon, no additional gases needed |
| 2. Application of the silicone elastomer solution | Dip‑coating bath or spray system (lab‑scale dip tank or air‑brush) | • Solution temperature: 30–100 °C (room temp to 80 °C is common for viscous silicone solutions)
• Immersion time: 10–30 s per dip
• Atmosphere during application: ambient air; no special pressure control required |
| 3. Solvent removal / initial curing | Conventional laboratory oven or hot‑plate with forced convection | • Temperature: 120 °C (typical for silicone elastomer cross‑linking)
• Time: 10 min per cycle
• Atmosphere: ambient air; optional inert gas if volatile solvent sensitivity is a concern |
| 4. Optional additional coating cycles | Repeat dip or spray as needed to reach desired thickness | • Same parameters as step 2 and 3 |
| 5. Final cure / post‑cure | Curing oven (if higher temperature required) | • Temperature: up to 180–200 °C if the silicone formulation requires a higher cross‑linking temperature
• Time: 10–30 min depending on thickness
• Atmosphere: ambient air; inert atmosphere can be used for sensitive formulations |

These parameters are drawn from the patent examples that describe coating silicone substrates with thermoplastic polyurethane or silicone solutions. The plasma treatment, dip/spray application, and oven curing steps are standard for medical‑grade elastomer coatings and are applicable to a PPS substrate because PPS is chemically inert and can accept surface activation by plasma followed by solvent‑based silicone coating.

**Key evidence**:
- Plasma treatment conditions (argon, 70 W, 60 s, atmospheric pressure) – Document 1, 4
- Solution application temperature range (30–100 °C) – Document 2
- Oven curing temperatures (120 °C to 200 °C) – Document 3, 4

question_answers.question: What specific crosslinking chemistry (peroxide, condensation, UV/VUV, or plasma treatment) is recommended for the silicone elastomer in this composite, including curing time, temperature range, and any necessary post‑treatment steps to achieve the required mechanical flexibility, optical clarity, and low gas permeability while ensuring no PFAS contamination?
question_answers.answer:
For a medical‑grade silicone elastomer coating on Toray Torelina PPS resin the industry standard is to cure the fluorosilicone by an **organic peroxide cross‑linking** route.  Typical practice uses dicumyl or di‑tert‑butyl peroxide in the range of 1–3 phr, with a cure cycle of **5–10 min at 115–170 °C (239–338 °F)**.  After this primary cure a short **post‑cure** step—often 20–30 min at ~200 °C—is performed to drive off any residual peroxide decomposition products and to complete the cross‑linking, which improves long‑term heat aging and maintains optical clarity.  No PFAS‑containing curing agents are required; the peroxide system is non‑PFAS and compatible with FDA/ISO biocompatibility requirements.  If surface adhesion or friction reduction is needed, a **plasma treatment** (e.g., low‑pressure H₂ or Ar plasma) can be applied after the post‑cure, but it does not affect bulk cross‑linking or gas barrier performance.

This peroxide‑based cure delivers the required mechanical flexibility, optical transparency and low gas permeability while avoiding PFAS contamination.

question_answers.question: Which safety precautions, material handling protocols, and regulatory (FDA, REACH, ISO 10993/USP Class VI) compliance tests must be performed for both the PPS resin and the silicone coating during lab‑scale synthesis to confirm biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and adherence to the specified performance thresholds?
question_answers.answer:
For a lab‑scale synthesis of Toray Torelina PPS resin with a medical‑grade silicone elastomer coating, the following safety and regulatory framework should be followed:

**Safety precautions & handling protocols (both materials)**
1. **Personal protective equipment (PPE)** – laboratory coat or apron, chemical‑resistant gloves, eye protection, and respiratory protection if volatile decomposition products are expected during melt processing or curing.  [Document 11]
2. **Ventilation** – conduct all heating, extrusion, or coating steps in a fume hood or well‑ventilated area to keep airborne contaminants below occupational exposure limits.  [Document 12]
3. **Temperature control & monitoring** – PPS resin melts above ~260 °C; silicone coatings are applied at 60–100 °C (or via plasma/PECVD).  Maintain process temperatures within the specified ranges and record them for traceability.  [Document 13], [Document 18]
4. **Closed‑container storage** – keep raw PPS pellets and silicone coating solutions in sealed containers to prevent moisture uptake or contamination, and store at 23 °C/50 % RH unless otherwise required.  [Document 3]
5. **Batch traceability & documentation** – assign lot numbers, record processing parameters, and maintain a laboratory notebook for each synthesis batch.  [Document 1], [Document 2]
6. **Fire & explosion safety** – avoid ignition sources near monomer or solvent vapors used in silicone surface treatments; ensure proper grounding of equipment.  [Document 23]

**Regulatory compliance tests (lab‑scale)**
| Material | Key ISO/USP tests | FDA/REACH considerations |
|---|---|---|
| PPS resin | • ISO 10993‑1 (general requirements) 
• ISO 10993‑5 (in‑vitro cytotoxicity)
• ISO 10993‑6 (implantation, if intended for implantable use)
• ISO 10993‑10 (irritation/sensitization)
• USP Class VI (systemic and intracutaneous toxicity) | • FDA 21 CFR 177 (medical device materials) – confirm that the resin is listed or has a pre‑market notification.
• REACH registration if the PPS monomer or additive is a regulated substance. |
| Silicone coating | • ISO 10993‑5 (cytotoxicity of the finished coated part)
• ISO 10993‑10 (skin irritation/sensitization) – especially for lubricious surface coatings.
• USP Class VI (if the coated device will be implanted or used in contact with tissue).
• ASTM D4239 (chemical resistance to common disinfectants) – to confirm inertness of the coating.
• FDA 21 CFR 177 – ensure silicone elastomer is approved for medical‑device use and that any additives are compliant. |

**Additional tests for chemical inertness & performance thresholds**
- **Thermal stability**: DSC/TGA to verify melting point and degradation temperature of PPS; silicone curing profile.
- **Mechanical testing**: tensile strength, elongation at break, hardness (Shore A) for the coated part – compare against product specifications.
- **Surface characterization**: contact angle measurement to confirm lubricity; FTIR or XPS to verify coating composition and adhesion.

These tests collectively provide evidence of biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and that the material meets the required performance criteria for medical‑device applications.

decomposition.composition_notes: The composite consists of a thin (≈10–20 µm) Toray Torelina polyphenylene sulfide (PPS) core with an overcoat of medical‑grade silicone elastomer applied by lamination or coextrusion. The silicone layer is cross‑linked, providing elasticity and barrier properties while representing only ~1–5 wt% of the final part due to its thinness. PPS provides the bulk mechanical strength and chemical resistance; silicone supplies low gas permeability and optical clarity.

retrieval_query_plan.query: Current US market availability and major suppliers of Toray Torelina PPS resin for industrial use.
Optimal processing temperature range and melt flow index specifications for Toray Torelina PPS resin in extrusion applications.
FDA approval status and REACH registration details for Toray Torelina PPS resin used in medical device manufacturing.
Major commercial suppliers of FDA-approved medical-grade silicone elastomer suitable for thin coating applications.
Recommended extrusion or lamination temperature range and crosslinking conditions for medical-grade silicone elastomer used as a coating layer.
ISO 10993 biocompatibility compliance documentation and safety data sheet information for medical-grade silicone elastomer.
Chemical compatibility assessment between Toray Torelina PPS resin and medical-grade silicone elastomer during lamination or coextrusion processes.
Recommended sequence and parameters for laminating a 10–20 µm PPS core with a crosslinked silicone coating to achieve barrier performance.
Optimal lamination pressure, temperature, and dwell time settings for bonding Toray Torelina PPS resin to medical-grade silicone elastomer without degrading either material.
Coextrusion extrusion rates, die design considerations, and cooling profiles required to produce a composite with a 10–20 µm PPS core and <50 µm silicone coating.
